skill.name: bilig-workpaper
skill.description: Use Bilig WorkPaper state to build, edit, validate, and export workbook-style formulas through Node.js, MCP tools, and local files.
skill.summary: Run workbook formulas through headless WorkPaper state without Excel UI automation.
skill.author: proompteng

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported shell execution, command substitution, hardcoded URLs, and weak cryptography indicators across SKILL.md and duplicate SKILL.txt. Review found the command-related tokens are in safety guidance, JSON argument-array examples, TypeScript examples, or markdown tool names, and the URLs are documentation links. No prompt injection, credential collection, data upload, or cryptographic implementation was found.

low_findings.title: External command templates are documented with safer argument arrays
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE. Static analysis flagged backticks, command substitution text, npm exec, MCP tool names, and TypeScript examples as shell execution. The skill explicitly warns not to concatenate user text into shell commands and recommends command plus args arrays or direct TypeScript calls. The npm examples still require normal package trust review before execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged shell metacharacters are presented as rejected input characters, and command examples use JSON args arrays. Legitimate external command execution is described, but there is no shell concatenation or hidden execution in the skill text.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs are reference documentation links
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE. Static analysis flagged project URLs as network indicators. The lines are a Reference URLs list pointing to documentation, compatibility notes, and the repository, with no instruction to upload workbook contents or send secrets.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are plainly labeled references and use the project documentation domain or GitHub repository. No request code, exfiltration endpoint, or automated network call is present.

low_findings.title: Weak cryptography detector matched non-cryptographic text
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE. Static analysis reported weak cryptographic algorithm indicators at the frontmatter description and the exclusion guidance. These lines describe WorkPaper use and unsupported Office features; they do not implement hashing, encryption, or signature logic.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The exact locations contain descriptive prose, not cryptographic code or algorithm selection. The repository files in scope are markdown skill instructions only.

content.user_title: Run workbook formulas without Excel UI
content.value_statement: Formula-driven workbooks are hard to automate when agents must drive Excel or a browser UI. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code to use Bilig WorkPaper state through local files, TypeScript, and MCP tools.
actual_capabilities: Configures the Bilig WorkPaper MCP server with command and args arrays.
Reads sheets, ranges, cells, display values, and exported WorkPaper documents.
Writes cell contents and verifies recalculated dependent outputs.
Shows direct TypeScript use of @bilig/headless WorkPaper APIs.
Generates local XLSX formula clinic reports for reduced bug fixtures.
Defines proof requirements for edited cells, before values, after values, and persistence.
limitations: Does not automate Excel desktop, Office macros, VBA, pivot tables, or charts.
Does not promise exact Excel behavior unless an explicit comparison is requested.
Requires local package execution or a host that can run a stdio MCP server.
Users should provide reduced public fixtures instead of private customer spreadsheets.

use_cases.title: Price model automation
use_cases.description: Run quote, budget, pricing, or payout formulas in local WorkPaper files and verify the output cells after edits.

use_cases.title: Agent spreadsheet tools
use_cases.description: Expose deterministic sheet and cell operations to an MCP client without relying on browser spreadsheet automation.

use_cases.title: Formula bug reduction
use_cases.description: Create a local report for a reduced XLSX formula or import issue using selected cells and compatibility notes.

prompt_templates.title: Read Workbook Outputs
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner check of existing workbook values
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Bilig WorkPaper MCP tools to list sheets, read the requested input cells, and read the dependent output cells. Report sheet names, A1 cells, and displayed values.

prompt_templates.title: Update Inputs Safely
prompt_templates.scenario: Edit a few inputs and verify recalculation
prompt_templates.prompt: Open the WorkPaper file through MCP. Change these input cells, then read each dependent output cell. Include before values, after values, and export proof.

prompt_templates.title: Embed WorkPaper Logic
prompt_templates.scenario: Use direct TypeScript in a service or test
prompt_templates.prompt: Use @bilig/headless directly in TypeScript to build a workbook from sheets, set one input value, read the calculated result, and serialize the exported WorkPaper state.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare Formula Clinic Report
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the local formula clinic flow for this reduced XLSX fixture. Inspect the named cells, summarize unsupported behavior, and avoid uploading workbook contents.

output_examples.input: Update Customers on the Inputs sheet from 20 to 32 and verify Revenue.
output_examples.output: The response identifies Inputs!B2 as edited, shows the prior and new customer values, reads Summary!B2 after recalculation, and notes exported WorkPaper persistence evidence.

output_examples.input: Check whether a reduced XLSX file has a formula import issue in Summary!B7.
output_examples.output: The response describes a local formula clinic report, names the inspected cells, explains any compatibility limitation, and avoids sharing private workbook data.

output_examples.input: Build a backend calculation from two input metrics and one revenue formula.
output_examples.output: The response outlines a WorkPaper with Inputs and Summary sheets, confirms the formula result, and states how the serialized document can be stored.

best_practices: Use MCP command and args arrays or direct TypeScript APIs when paths or cells come from users.
Read dependent output cells after every write and include before and after values in the response.
Use reduced public fixtures for formula bug reports and keep private spreadsheets local.
anti_patterns: Do not build shell commands by concatenating workbook paths, formulas, or cell references.
Do not claim Excel desktop compatibility for macros, charts, pivots, or COM automation.
Do not report a workbook update without exported state or readback proof.

faq.question: Does this skill require Microsoft Excel?
faq.answer: No. It uses Bilig WorkPaper state and @bilig/headless for formula work without driving Excel UI.

faq.question: Can it edit workbook files through an agent?
faq.answer: Yes, when the host can run the MCP stdio server and the WorkPaper file is configured as writable.

faq.question: Does it upload spreadsheets?
faq.answer: No evidence found in the skill instructions. The formula clinic section says reports are local and should not upload workbook contents.

faq.question: What should the agent verify after a cell write?
faq.answer: It should report the exact sheet and cell, before values, recalculated after values, and exported or serialized persistence proof.

faq.question: Can it handle every Excel feature?
faq.answer: No. The skill excludes macros, VBA, pivots, charts, COM automation, and exact Excel desktop behavior unless comparison is requested.

faq.question: Why does the audit mention command risk?
faq.answer: The skill documents npm exec and MCP command templates. They use safer args arrays, but users still need normal trust review for package execution.
